general_concerns: Because his father had similar attacks, he believes cluster headaches are unavoidable and that treatment can’t really prevent them—only “wait them out.”
He thinks the attacks are caused by “sinus problems” or seasonal allergies because the pain is around one eye and he gets tearing and nasal congestion.
He believes strong painkillers (e.g., opioids) are the best option for these headaches and is skeptical that oxygen or migraine-style medicines could work fast enough.
He thinks frequent use of triptans will “damage the brain” or cause addiction, so he sometimes delays taking acute medication until the pain is severe.
He assumes that if a CT scan was normal in the past, there is no need for further evaluation even if the pattern changes.
economic_concerns: He worries high-flow oxygen at home will be expensive and complicated to obtain through insurance and fears it will increase his monthly bills.
He is concerned that newer injectable or nasal treatments will have high copays, so he tries to ration medications to make them last longer.
He worries that taking time off during cluster periods could threaten his job performance and income, so he minimizes symptoms at work.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male IT support specialist. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Open with: “I keep getting these brutal one-sided headaches that hit out of nowhere, mostly at night.” Early in the interview, volunteer: severe stabbing pain behind the left eye/temple in attacks lasting 45–60 minutes; occurs in clusters for a few weeks then months of no headaches; left eye waters/turns red; left nostril runs or feels blocked; you can’t sit still (pace/rock); your dad had similar headaches. If asked, add: wakes you 1–2 hours after sleep; almost always same side; little nausea; light/sound not a big issue; alcohol triggers during bad stretches; 1–3 attacks/day; OTC meds barely help. Do not mention unless specifically asked about eye/face changes: mild eyelid droop, facial sweating/flushing, smaller pupil, panic/restlessness, seasonal pattern. Be reluctant/embarrassed unless asked about substances: you smoke more during flares and use a couple drinks to sleep even though it can trigger attacks. Mention misconception: brief electric-shock facial twinge before the headache makes you worry it’s a nerve problem; sometimes chewing/brushing triggers a twinge. If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and admit worry about oxygen/injectables being expensive and missing work.

medications: Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily
Nicotine polacrilex gum 2 mg buccal q2h PRN cravings (max 24 pieces/day)
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO q6h PRN pain (uses during headaches)
confounders: Tobacco use (can worsen headaches and overlaps with common cluster headache demographics)
Alcohol intake during headache bouts triggers attacks (may be misattributed to 'hangover' headaches)
Nasal congestion/rhinorrhea during attacks can be mistaken for sinusitis
Intermittent unilateral tearing and eye redness can be mistaken for allergic conjunctivitis
Frequent NSAID use for severe pain may obscure typical timing/pattern and raises medication-overuse concern

correct_diagnosis.primary: Cluster headache (familial)
required_evidence: Severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes
Attack frequency 1 every other day to 8 per day during a cluster period

Ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms (e.g., lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, facial sweating, miosis/ptosis) and/or marked restlessness/agitation during attacks
Recurring bouts (cluster periods) with remission or chronic pattern
Family history of cluster headache (supports but not required)
No red flags suggesting secondary headache (e.g., new neuro deficit, thunderclap onset, fever/meningismus)

medications: Oxygen 100% 12-15 L/min non-rebreather mask at onset for 15-20 minutes PRN attacks
Sumatriptan 6 mg subcutaneous at onset; may repeat once after 1 hour (max 12 mg/24 h)
Verapamil 80 mg PO three times daily; titrate every 7-14 days as needed/tolerated (typical effective total daily dose 240-480 mg/day) with ECG monitoring
Prednisone 60 mg PO daily for 5 days then taper by 10 mg every 2 days (bridge/transition while verapamil titrated)
non_pharmacological: Avoid alcohol during cluster period (common trigger)
Maintain regular sleep schedule; avoid sleep deprivation
Keep a headache diary (timing, duration, autonomic features, triggers, response to therapy)
Consider neurology/headache specialist referral for confirmation and preventive titration; consider alternative preventives (e.g., galcanezumab for episodic cluster) if inadequate response
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks for response and verapamil titration/ECG review; sooner (24-72 hours) if attacks remain uncontrolled or adverse effects occur

must_include: Cluster headache is a primary headache disorder causing short, very severe one-sided attacks with autonomic symptoms
How it differs from trigeminal neuralgia (TN pain is brief electric-shock-like seconds, triggered by touch/chewing; cluster lasts minutes-hours with tearing/nasal symptoms and restlessness)
Expected course: attacks come in clusters over weeks-months with remissions (or chronic pattern)
Acute treatment plan: high-flow oxygen and/or subcutaneous sumatriptan at onset
Preventive plan: verapamil as first-line; needs dose titration and ECG monitoring for heart block/bradycardia
Bridge therapy: short steroid taper to reduce attacks while preventive takes effect
When to seek urgent care: new neurologic deficits, worst thunderclap headache, fever/neck stiffness, chest pain/shortness of breath after triptan, persistent vomiting/dehydration
Review cardiovascular history and contraindications to triptans (CAD, stroke/TIA, uncontrolled hypertension, hemiplegic/basilar migraine) and verapamil (heart block, severe bradycardia, HFrEF)
Discuss common side effects: triptan (chest/neck tightness, flushing), verapamil (constipation, dizziness, edema, bradycardia), prednisone (insomnia, mood changes, hyperglycemia, GI upset)
Confirm penicillin allergy is not relevant to chosen therapies but should remain documented
Avoid alcohol during cluster periods and identify personal triggers
Adherence importance for preventive therapy and monitoring (ECG, dose adjustments)
Use of headache diary to guide treatment effectiveness and pattern recognition

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit headache features to confirm cluster phenotype and screen for secondary/red-flag causes (timing, duration, laterality, autonomic symptoms, restlessness, triggers incl alcohol, frequency/cluster pattern, family history, neuro symptoms, fever/neck stiffness, thunderclap onset).

Assess comorbidities and medication history relevant to therapy selection (cardiovascular history, BP control, stroke/TIA, pregnancy status if relevant, current meds/OTC/supplements, prior triptan/verapamil exposure, steroid risks such as diabetes/ulcer).
State working diagnosis of familial cluster headache and briefly justify based on collected criteria; note differential (trigeminal neuralgia) and absence/presence of red flags.
Perform required safety check prior to prescribing; verify allergy list (including penicillin) and confirm no relevant allergies to proposed therapies.
Perform required drug interaction check prior to prescribing (e.g., triptan with ergotamines/other triptans within 24h; serotonergic drugs; verapamil with beta-blockers/digoxin/CYP3A4 inhibitors).

Complete contraindication screening prior to prescribing (triptan: CAD, vasospasm, stroke/TIA, uncontrolled HTN, hemiplegic/basilar migraine; verapamil: heart block, severe bradycardia, HFrEF; steroid cautions).

Initiate evidence-based acute and preventive therapy plan: high-flow oxygen and/or subcutaneous sumatriptan for attacks; verapamil preventive with titration plan; short steroid taper as bridge if appropriate.
Order baseline and follow-up ECG monitoring for verapamil titration (and vitals/BP as needed); consider additional tests only if red flags or comorbidity prompts.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis vs trigeminal neuralgia; expected course; explain acute/preventive/bridge treatments and monitoring; side effects; urgent-care warning signs; lifestyle trigger avoidance and headache diary.
Arrange timely follow-up for verapamil titration and ECG review (e.g., within 1–2 weeks) and reassess attack frequency, medication tolerance, and safety.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 44-year-old woman, cooperative and clear. Start by saying: “I’ve had burning when I pee and I keep needing to go, like every 20 minutes.” Volunteer: burning with urination, frequent small amounts, sudden strong urge, and lower belly pressure/discomfort.

If asked about urine: say it’s a little cloudy and smells stronger; you saw a few pink streaks once or twice; it hurts most at the very end. If asked about fever/back pain: deny fever, flank/back/side pain.

Do NOT volunteer at first: you’ve been holding urine at work, drinking less water, used a new scented body wash/bubble bath, and had sex a couple days before onset and didn’t pee after.

Only if asked about sexual history or vaginal symptoms, reluctantly share: unprotected sex recently, unusual discharge and irritation; you’re worried about an STI.

Mention misconceptions: you think it might be a yeast infection due to mild itch; cranberry pills haven’t helped.

Express cost/time concerns: ask for minimum necessary tests, generic meds, and a plan that avoids multiple visits. If the clinician shows empathy, thank them and become more open.

medications: Metformin ER 1000 mg PO twice daily
Lisinopril 10 mg PO once daily
Sertraline 50 mg PO once daily

confounders: Recent self-treatment with leftover cephalexin for 2 days (may partially treat infection and affect culture/symptoms)
Poor glycemic control with intermittent hyperglycemia increasing infection risk and blunting typical symptoms

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute gastroenteritis with dehydration (hypovolemic hyponatremia)
required_evidence: History of acute onset vomiting and/or diarrhea with reduced oral intake
Clinical signs of dehydration (e.g., tachycardia, orthostasis, dry mucous membranes, decreased urine output)
Basic metabolic panel consistent with volume depletion (elevated BUN and/or low bicarbonate) and/or hyponatremia consistent with hypovolemia
No features requiring alternative primary diagnosis (e.g., no focal peritonitis, no severe GI bleeding, no persistent high fever with systemic toxicity)

expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (stress leukocytosis possible)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Normal to mildly elevated from hemoconcentration
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Low to low-normal (mild hyponatremia consistent with hypovolemia)
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Low-normal to low (GI losses) or normal
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Low-normal; may be low with vomiting
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low (metabolic acidosis from diarrhea) or high if predominant vomiting; overall expected trend toward low with diarrheal illness

medications: Oral rehydration solution 250-500 mL PO after each loose stool (or small frequent sips as tolerated)
Ondansetron 4 mg ODT/PO every 8 hours as needed for nausea/vomiting
non_pharmacological: Assess dehydration severity; if unable to tolerate PO or signs of moderate/severe dehydration, give isotonic IV fluids (0.9% normal saline bolus) and reassess
Resume age-appropriate diet as tolerated; avoid high-sugar drinks/alcohol; continue hydration
Return precautions for worsening symptoms (persistent vomiting, inability to keep fluids down, severe abdominal pain, blood in stool, high fever, confusion, fainting, minimal urine output)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Re-evaluate within 24-48 hours (sooner same day/ED if worsening, unable to hydrate, or severe dehydration).

must_include: Likely acute infectious gastroenteritis causing fluid loss and dehydration
Explain what dehydration means and why labs (e.g., BUN, sodium, bicarbonate) can change with fluid loss
Discuss red flags that would suggest a different or more serious cause
Primary treatment is rehydration (oral preferred if tolerated; IV if not)
How to use oral rehydration solution (small frequent sips; replace ongoing losses)
Ondansetron is for symptom control to help keep fluids down; review dosing and when to stop
Review allergies (penicillin and shellfish) and reassure that planned treatment avoids these triggers
Set expectations for symptom course (typically improves over 1-3 days depending on cause)
When to seek urgent care (worsening dehydration, blood in stool, severe pain, persistent fever, confusion/fainting)
Hand hygiene and food safety to reduce transmission/recurrence
Diet guidance during recovery (bland foods as tolerated; avoid high-sugar beverages)
Hydration goals and monitoring (urine output, dizziness, thirst)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history for acute GI illness and dehydration severity (onset/duration, vomiting/diarrhea frequency, oral intake, urine output, dizziness/syncope, fever, abdominal pain, blood/mucus, sick contacts, travel/food exposure, pregnancy possibility, comorbidities, current meds).
Order basic dehydration and electrolyte assessment (e.g., BMP/CMP for sodium, potassium, bicarbonate/CO2, BUN/Cr; consider glucose; +/- CBC if fever or concern for systemic illness).
Interpret labs for dehydration and complications (pre-renal azotemia, electrolyte derangements, metabolic acidosis/alkalosis) to guide oral vs IV rehydration and safety of antiemetics.
Assign likely diagnosis consistent with L1 case: acute infectious gastroenteritis with dehydration; note differential and red flags requiring alternative workup.
Critical safety check before prescribing; confirm allergies (penicillin, shellfish) and reaction types; ensure planned meds/fluids do not contain allergens.
Non-critical check before prescribing; screen for interactions (e.g., QT-prolonging agents if considering ondansetron; serotonergic meds; other antiemetics).

Initiate treatment plan: oral rehydration solution instructions; prescribe ondansetron for nausea/vomiting if needed to tolerate fluids; choose IV fluids if unable to keep down PO or if moderate/severe dehydration.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and dehydration/lab changes; explain rehydration and ondansetron use; address allergy concerns and expected course; provide red flags; lifestyle/food safety and diet guidance; hydration monitoring.
Arrange follow-up timeframe based on severity (e.g., 24–48h if ongoing symptoms or abnormal labs; sooner/ED if red flags) and provide contingency plan.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, questions, and safety-net instructions.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Insomnia disorder
required_evidence: Difficulty initiating sleep and/or maintaining sleep and/or early morning awakening with inability to return to sleep

Frequency at least 3 nights per week
Duration at least 3 months for chronic insomnia (or <3 months for short-term insomnia)
No evidence that symptoms are better explained by another sleep-wake disorder, substance/medication effect, or untreated medical/psychiatric condition

Screen for psychotic symptoms to evaluate differential of early-onset schizophrenia (hallucinations, delusions, disorganized thought/behavior, negative symptoms) and assess safety (suicidality/violence)

expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Low or low-normal if comorbid iron deficiency/restless legs is contributing; otherwise within reference range

expected_lab_results.MCV: Normal to low (microcytosis if iron deficiency present)
expected_lab_results.Serum Iron: Low if iron deficiency present; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.TIBC: High if iron deficiency present; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Transferrin Saturation: Low (<20%) if iron deficiency present; otherwise normal

Elicit patient goals and concerns about sleep and mental health
Discuss warning signs requiring urgent help: suicidal thoughts, hallucinations, paranoia, severe agitation, inability to care for self

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit insomnia phenotype (onset vs maintenance vs early awakening), duration, frequency, daytime impairment, precipitating stressors, and sleep schedule to confirm diagnostic criteria and guide CBT-I vs medication choice.

Screen for medical/psychiatric and substance contributors: depression/anxiety, mania/hypomania, psychotic symptoms (hallucinations, paranoia, disorganization), suicidality, pain, OSA symptoms, RLS symptoms, caffeine/nicotine/alcohol/cannabis/stimulants, and supplement use including choline.
Order thyroid studies and iron studies (e.g., TSH/free T4; ferritin/iron panel) when clinically indicated to rule out contributors such as thyroid disease and iron deficiency/restless legs.
Review lab results to refine differential and ensure no reversible contributor is missed before finalizing the plan.
Make a working diagnosis (e.g., insomnia disorder) or identify an alternate/secondary cause requiring different management or urgent evaluation.
Explain diagnosis and differential, including rationale for thyroid/iron tests and why screening for psychotic symptoms matters (early psychosis), and provide urgent warning signs.

Provide CBT-I and sleep hygiene counseling: stimulus control (consistent wake time; bed only for sleep/sex), sleep restriction principles (guided), caffeine timing, screen/light management, and relaxation routine.
Before any prescribing, confirm medication allergies/intolerances to prevent serious adverse reactions (critical safety check).
Before any prescribing, check for drug-drug and drug-substance interactions (including alcohol, sedatives, cannabis, supplements) (critical safety check).

Before any prescribing, assess contraindications (e.g., pregnancy risk, severe hepatic impairment, untreated OSA, history of complex sleep behaviors with Z-drugs, bipolar/mania risk, glaucoma/urinary retention risk for anticholinergic meds) (critical safety check).

If medication is appropriate, select agent matched to sleep-onset vs maintenance issues; counsel on lowest effective dose/shortest duration and avoiding alcohol/other sedatives; review common side effects (e.g., next-day sedation; anticholinergic effects with higher-dose doxepin).
Arrange follow-up to assess response, adherence to CBT-I, side effects, and evolving psychiatric symptoms; earlier follow-up if safety concerns.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.chief_complaint: I keep getting these brutal one-sided headaches around my eye that come in attacks.

I don’t usually feel nauseated or throw up with these attacks

Between attacks, I feel completely normal—no lingering headache

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old anxious but clear-speaking male HVAC technician. Open with: “I keep getting brutal one-sided headaches around my left eye in attacks.” Early in the visit, volunteer: sudden severe stabbing pain behind the left eye; occurs in clusters for a few weeks then months fine; left eye red/tearing; left nostril stuffy/runny; you can’t sit still (pace/rock); dad had similar headaches. If asked, add: attacks last 30–90 minutes; often wake you at the same time at night; 1–3/day during bad stretches; little light/sound sensitivity; usually no nausea/vomiting; occasional droopy eyelid/smaller pupil; alcohol triggers during cluster periods; oxygen helped once. Do NOT mention unless directly asked: pre-attack “on edge” feeling; fear of sleep; increased caffeine; between attacks normal; sweating/flushing on painful side; brief electric-shock facial pains/“trigeminal neuralgia.” Resist sharing unless asked about safety/substances: more smoking/drinking, used a friend’s pain pills, fleeting thoughts like “I can’t live like this.” Express misconceptions: sinus/tooth problem, dark room didn’t help, worried about brain tumor. If clinician is empathetic, you soften, admit fear and cost concerns about triptans/oxygen/preventives and follow-up costs.

medications: Carbamazepine 200 mg PO twice daily
Sumatriptan 6 mg SC as needed at headache onset (max 2 doses/24 h)
Verapamil ER 240 mg PO daily
Oxygen 100% via non-rebreather 12-15 L/min for 15-20 minutes as needed for acute attacks
Prednisone 60 mg PO daily x 5 days then taper over 10 days (bridge therapy during cluster period)
Melatonin 10 mg PO nightly
confounders: Trigeminal neuralgia causing overlapping unilateral facial pain
Medication-overuse risk from frequent triptan use during cluster periods
Alcohol as a trigger during active cluster bouts
Possible sleep-disordered breathing (snoring, daytime sleepiness) contributing to nocturnal attacks
Intermittent nicotine use (vaping) associated with cluster headache prevalence
Verapamil and carbamazepine interaction (CYP3A4) complicating dose titration and adverse-effect attribution

correct_diagnosis.primary: Cluster headache (familial/episodic pattern)
acceptable_alternatives: Chronic cluster headache (if attacks occur for >1 year without remission or with remission <3 months)
required_evidence: Severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes when untreated
Attack frequency from 1 every other day up to 8/day, often in clusters over weeks with remissions (episodic) or minimal remission (chronic)

At least one ipsilateral autonomic symptom during attacks (conjunctival injection/lacrimation, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, forehead/facial sweating, miosis/ptosis) and/or marked restlessness/agitation

No focal neurologic deficits on exam and no red flags suggesting secondary headache (e.g., sudden thunderclap onset, persistent new neurologic signs, fever/meningismus, immunosuppression, cancer, age >50 new headache)

expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Normal (needed before lithium if considered; also for general safety)
expected_lab_results.ESR: Normal (helps reduce concern for giant cell arteritis if clinically considered)

medications: Oxygen 100% 12-15 L/min via non-rebreather mask for 15-20 minutes at attack onset PRN
Sumatriptan 6 mg subcutaneous at attack onset; may repeat once after 1 hour (max 12 mg/24 h)
Verapamil immediate-release 80 mg PO three times daily; titrate every 1-2 weeks as tolerated toward 240-480 mg/day (with ECG monitoring)
Prednisone 60 mg PO daily for 5 days, then taper by 10 mg every 2 days to stop (bridge while verapamil is titrated)
non_pharmacological: Avoid alcohol during cluster periods (common trigger)
Maintain regular sleep schedule; avoid sleep deprivation
Headache diary (timing, duration, triggers, response to abortives)
Consider referral to neurology/headache specialist for confirmation and preventive titration; consider imaging if atypical features or first presentation
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks to assess response, adverse effects, and titrate preventive therapy; sooner/urgent if red flags or worsening pattern

must_include: Cluster headache is a primary headache disorder with short, very severe unilateral attacks and autonomic symptoms/restlessness
Familial tendency can occur; it is not dangerous by itself but can be disabling
Discuss expected course (clusters over weeks with remissions vs chronic pattern) and when to seek urgent care (new neurologic deficits, fever, sudden worst headache)
Acute/abortive plan: high-flow oxygen and/or subcutaneous sumatriptan at attack onset
Preventive plan: verapamil daily to reduce attack frequency; may require dose titration
Bridge therapy: short steroid taper to reduce attacks while preventive takes effect

Key safety points: do not exceed triptan daily limits; avoid triptans with uncontrolled hypertension, ischemic heart disease, prior stroke/TIA, or hemiplegic/basilar migraine; verapamil can cause constipation, low blood pressure, bradycardia
Confirm and document allergies (penicillin urticarial rash; naproxen angioedema) and advise avoiding naproxen/NSAID use without clinician guidance given angioedema history

Review cardiovascular history and current medications to ensure triptan and verapamil safety (including avoiding strong CYP3A4 inhibitors with verapamil and avoiding ergotamine within 24 hours of triptans)
Set expectations: oxygen often works quickly; preventive therapy may take days to weeks and may need adjustments
Avoid alcohol and other personal triggers during active cluster periods
Sleep regularity and stress management
Use a headache diary to guide treatment titration and identify triggers
Provide clear return precautions and follow-up plan

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history: anemia symptoms, fever pattern/periodicity, infection history, developmental milestones, family history/consanguinity, prior transfusions, diet/supplements, medication list, and red flags needing urgent care.

Interpret results to confirm underproduction anemia pattern (low/normal retic for degree of anemia), assess iron status/overload risk, inflammation, and screen for immune deficits; identify urgency (severe anemia, neutropenia, high inflammatory markers with systemic illness).

Synthesize congenital syndrome diagnosis and explicitly consider/contrast differentials (cyclic hematopoiesis; inflammatory arthritis/autoinflammatory syndromes) based on periodicity, neutrophil cycling, inflammatory markers, joint findings, and infection history; note need for specialist/genetic testing and possible marrow evaluation for ring sideroblasts.

Explain diagnosis and rationale for further testing; provide fever/immunodeficiency safety plan; discuss transfusion thresholds conceptually and iron overload monitoring; introduce pyridoxine trial concept and monitoring plan.
Critical safety check before any prescribing: verify and document allergies (amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash; TMP-SMX nausea/vomiting) and reaction types/severity.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing: review current meds/supplements for interactions with proposed therapy (e.g., pyridoxine, antipyretics, iron chelation if later needed).

Initiate appropriate treatment plan consistent with workup: trial of pyridoxine for congenital sideroblastic anemia with clear dose range per age/weight policy, monitoring plan (repeat CBC/reticulocytes), and symptomatic fever management plan (antipyretics) while avoiding contraindicated/allergenic antibiotics.

Arrange close follow-up for lab review and response assessment (CBC/retic trend), plus referrals/coordination with hematology and immunology; set earlier review if fever episodes recur or labs are concerning.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, providing return precautions, and ensuring follow-up plan is clear.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5, autosomal recessive (AR HIES; DOCK8 deficiency)
acceptable_alternatives: PGM3 deficiency (congenital glycosylation disorder with hyper-IgE and infections)

Severe atopic dermatitis with secondary infection
required_evidence: Markedly elevated total IgE (often >2000 IU/mL) with recurrent sinopulmonary and/or skin infections
Peripheral eosinophilia (absolute and/or percent elevated)
Recurrent bacterial skin/respiratory infections and/or severe viral cutaneous infections (e.g., HSV, HPV, molluscum) suggestive of DOCK8 phenotype
Atopic disease history (eczema, food/environmental allergies) and/or failure to thrive
Exclusion of secondary causes of high IgE (parasitic infection, severe atopy alone) and other primary immunodeficiencies
Immunology confirmation plan: lymphocyte subsets, vaccine titers, IgG/IgA/IgM, and genetic testing for DOCK8 or related AR-HIES genes

expected_lab_results.Total IgE: Markedly elevated (often >2000 IU/mL; may be extremely high)
expected_lab_results.Eosinophils (absolute): Elevated (eosinophilia, commonly >0.5 x10^9/L; may be >1.5 x10^9/L)
expected_lab_results.WBC: Often normal to elevated; may be elevated during acute infection
expected_lab_results.Neutrophils (absolute): Normal to elevated during bacterial infection; not typically chronically neutropenic in DOCK8 deficiency
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Usually normal; may be low if chronic inflammation or frequent infections
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Usually normal; may be elevated as an acute phase reactant
expected_lab_results.CRP: Normal when well; elevated during active bacterial infection/inflammation

Acyclovir 400 mg PO BID for herpesvirus prophylaxis if history of recurrent HSV/VZV or significant viral skin disease

Cetirizine 10 mg PO daily for allergic symptoms/pruritus (as needed based on symptoms)
Triamcinolone 0.1% ointment apply thin layer to eczematous areas BID for up to 2 weeks, then taper/step-down
Mupirocin 2% ointment apply to localized impetiginized lesions TID for 5-7 days (if present)

Infection prevention: prompt evaluation for fevers/skin abscesses, hygiene measures, avoid sick contacts when feasible

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immunology follow-up within 1-2 weeks; sooner (24-72 hours) if currently symptomatic or if CRP/WBC elevated suggesting active infection

steps: ASK: assess acuity, infection pattern, eczema, exposures, vaccines, family history, meds/allergies

ORDER_LAB: CBC with differential (eosinophils), total IgE, CMP (renal/hepatic baseline), quantitative immunoglobulins, lymphocyte subsets, vaccine titers; consider HIV test if appropriate; obtain cultures if active infection present

PRESCRIBE: initiate antimicrobial prophylaxis (e.g., TMP-SMX if appropriate) and supportive eczema/skin infection prevention measures; specify dosing schedule conceptually and monitoring labs; create plan for prompt evaluation/cultures for breakthrough infections
EDUCATE: deliver all milestones (diagnosis, treatment, concerns including HSCT, lifestyle/vaccine guidance, red flags)

You are LiXX, a 52-year-old woman who speaks vaguely and is a bit evasive. Start with: “I can’t stop having watery diarrhea, like tea-colored water, and I feel like I’m drying out.” Volunteer only: many watery stools daily for weeks, very thirsty/lightheaded on standing, crampy belly better after diarrhea, weight loss from fear of eating, and feeling weak.

Resist admitting: accidents, avoiding work/social plans, and overusing OTC anti-diarrhea—share only if asked about impact or meds.

If the clinician shows empathy, soften slightly, but stay guarded and minimize details unless asked.

medications: Tranexamic acid 650 mg PO three times daily as needed for epistaxis
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
Pantoprazole 40 mg PO daily
confounders: Chronic iron deficiency anemia from HHT-related bleeding may mask dehydration severity (tachycardia, fatigue) and complicate interpretation of symptoms
Proton pump inhibitor use can partially reduce acid-related symptoms and obscure assessment of hypochlorhydria/achlorhydria associated with VIPoma
Recent use of over-the-counter magnesium-containing antacids/laxatives (intermittent) can mimic or worsen diarrhea and electrolyte losses
Possible GI bleeding from telangiectasias can coexist with diarrhea and weight loss, complicating attribution of symptoms

correct_diagnosis.primary: VIPoma (vasoactive intestinal peptide–secreting pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor) causing WDHA syndrome
acceptable_alternatives: Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor with VIP hypersecretion
WDHA syndrome (watery diarrhea, hypokalemia, achlorhydria) due to VIPoma
required_evidence: Fasting serum VIP markedly elevated (typically >200 pg/mL, often much higher) in the setting of secretory watery diarrhea
Electrolyte pattern consistent with secretory diarrhea: hypokalemia and non–anion gap metabolic acidosis (low bicarbonate) from bicarbonate loss
Clinical volume depletion from high-volume watery diarrhea; diarrhea persists with fasting (secretory pattern)

expected_lab_results.Serum VIP: Markedly elevated above diagnostic threshold (typically >200 pg/mL) on a fasting sample
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Often low or low-normal due to volume depletion and GI losses
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Often elevated relative to bicarbonate (hyperchloremia) consistent with non–anion gap metabolic acidosis
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low (metabolic acidosis from bicarbonate loss in stool)
expected_lab_results.Magnesium: Often low due to GI losses
expected_lab_results.Calcium (total): Usually normal; may be mildly abnormal depending on dehydration/albumin but not a defining feature

medications: 0.9% sodium chloride IV bolus 1-2 L then continuous infusion titrated to hemodynamics/urine output
Potassium chloride IV 10-20 mEq/hour with continuous ECG monitoring (or oral replacement if mild and tolerating PO), adjust to target K+ >4.0 mEq/L
Magnesium sulfate IV 1-2 g (8-16 mEq) over 1 hour, repeat as needed to correct hypomagnesemia
Octreotide 100-200 mcg subcutaneous every 8 hours (or continuous IV infusion 25-50 mcg/hour if severe), titrate to control diarrhea and reduce VIP
non_pharmacological: Strict intake/output monitoring; stool volume quantification; daily weights

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 22-year-old female college-educated elementary school teaching assistant. You are cooperative and communicate clearly. Start by saying: “I can’t get this bleeding to stop after a small cut.” Also volunteer early: you bruise very easily and have nosebleeds that take a long time to stop. If asked about bleeding history, share: heavy/long periods, gums bleeding with brushing/flossing, delayed re-bleeding after cuts, and that a tooth extraction bled again later that night and next day. If asked about joint issues, mention swelling/pain after a minor twist like it filled with blood. Do NOT mention unless directly asked: newborn belly-button bleeding, poor wound healing/scabs coming off, or the severe sudden headache/sleepiness after a minor head bump. Only reveal “resistant” topics (spotting after sex; avoiding dental care due to fear of bleeding) if the clinician asks specifically about gynecologic bleeding or dental avoidance. Express misconceptions: you think ibuprofen this week caused the bleeding and that your low-iron diet means “thin blood.” If costs come up, say prophylactic treatment seems optional and you’d prefer to stretch refills and only seek care when actively bleeding. If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and become more open.

medications: Factor XIII concentrate (recombinant) 35 IU/kg IV every 28 days
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
Tranexamic acid 1300 mg PO three times daily as needed for heavy menstrual bleeding (up to 5 days per cycle)
Levonorgestrel intrauterine device (52 mg) in place
confounders: Normal PT and aPTT despite clinically significant bleeding tendency
Heavy menstrual bleeding causing iron deficiency anemia and fatigue, potentially masking episodic bleeding severity
Recent NSAID use for dysmenorrhea (ibuprofen 400 mg PRN) increasing mucosal bleeding/bruising
Remote history of delayed post-dental-extraction bleeding (72 hours) that may be misattributed to poor local hemostasis rather than a coagulation disorder

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital factor XIII deficiency
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital FXIII deficiency with bleeding diathesis
required_evidence: Bleeding history suggestive of impaired clot stabilization (e.g., delayed re-bleeding after trauma/procedures, poor wound healing, umbilical stump bleeding in infancy, mucocutaneous bleeding, menorrhagia, recurrent pregnancy loss, or spontaneous intracranial hemorrhage)
Normal PT/INR, normal aPTT, normal thrombin time, and normal fibrinogen despite bleeding symptoms
Normal platelet count and indices on CBC (or anemia only if significant bleeding)
Recognition that routine coagulation studies can be normal in FXIII deficiency and that confirmation requires FXIII activity/antigen testing (not available in provided labs)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Often normal; may be decreased if there has been significant/recent bleeding (normocytic anemia typical unless chronic iron deficiency develops).
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hematocrit: Often normal; may be decreased if significant/recent bleeding.
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Typically normal (normocytic); may be low if chronic blood loss with iron deficiency.

medications: Factor XIII concentrate (recombinant catridecacog or plasma-derived FXIII) 35 IU/kg IV once for acute bleeding or peri-procedural management
Factor XIII concentrate prophylaxis 35 IU/kg IV every 28 days (long-term prophylaxis to prevent spontaneous bleeding, including intracranial hemorrhage)
non_pharmacological: Urgent hematology consultation for confirmation testing (FXIII activity/antigen) and ongoing prophylaxis plan
Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs and other agents that increase bleeding risk unless explicitly advised by hematology
Bleeding precautions and prompt evaluation for head trauma or neurologic symptoms (low threshold for emergent imaging due to ICH risk)
Genetic counseling and family screening as appropriate
Peri-procedural planning: coordinate with hematology for FXIII replacement before surgeries/dental work
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 1-2 weeks for confirmatory testing and prophylaxis initiation/optimization; sooner (same day) if active bleeding, planned procedure, pregnancy, or any neurologic symptoms.

must_include: Factor XIII helps stabilize clots; deficiency can cause delayed or recurrent bleeding even when PT/INR and aPTT are normal
Routine clotting tests being normal does not rule out a serious bleeding disorder in this condition
Need for specialized FXIII activity testing to confirm and to guide long-term management
FXIII replacement is the specific treatment (concentrate preferred); it prevents and treats bleeding
Prophylaxis is typically required because of risk of serious spontaneous bleeding (including intracranial hemorrhage)
When to seek emergency care (e.g., head injury, severe headache, neurologic symptoms, uncontrolled bleeding)
Review allergy history (penicillin, latex) and how care will avoid triggers (latex-free supplies)
Discuss potential risks of FXIII products (hypersensitivity reactions; rare thrombosis) and monitoring plan
Discuss implications for future procedures, menstruation, and pregnancy (need coordinated hematology/OB care)
Avoid medications that increase bleeding risk unless approved (aspirin/NSAIDs); check before starting new OTC/herbal agents
Carry a bleeding disorder/medical alert identification and an emergency plan
Inform all clinicians/dentists prior to procedures; plan FXIII dosing ahead of time

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit bleeding history and red flags (delayed bleeding, umbilical stump bleeding, intracranial bleed symptoms), family history/consanguinity, prior labs/treatments, and current meds/OTC/herbals to support suspected congenital FXIII deficiency and assess urgency.

Order specialized testing to confirm FXIII deficiency and establish baseline (FXIII activity assay; consider antigen level and inhibitor screen if low activity; CBC/fibrinogen/PT/INR/aPTT to document that routine tests may be normal).
Review results to confirm diagnosis (low FXIII activity with otherwise normal routine coagulation studies) and to guide treatment/prophylaxis planning.
Make the diagnosis of congenital factor XIII deficiency once supported by low FXIII activity and consistent clinical features.
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm allergies (including penicillin, latex) and plan avoidance (latex-free supplies) to reduce risk of hypersensitivity/exposure.
Noncritical safety check before prescribing: review current meds/OTC/herbals for agents that increase bleeding risk (e.g., aspirin/NSAIDs) and any relevant interactions with planned therapies.
Initiate specific therapy with FXIII replacement (concentrate preferred) for treatment/prophylaxis; include dosing plan/monitoring and emergency instructions for breakthrough bleeding.
Communicate diagnosis, rationale for specialized testing, treatment/prophylaxis and emergency precautions, risks/monitoring, and lifestyle/procedure planning.
Arrange hematology follow-up for long-term prophylaxis, monitoring FXIII activity, planning for procedures, and counseling for menstruation/pregnancy.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

minimal_paths.description: Straightforward outpatient evaluation confirming congenital FXIII deficiency with low FXIII activity and initiating FXIII concentrate prophylaxis with safety checks and education.

umbilical stump bleeding or neonatal bleeding history
history of intracranial hemorrhage or neurologic symptoms

pregnancy status/menstrual history or future pregnancy plans (if applicable)
steps: ASK: bleeding history, family history, meds/OTC/herbals, red flags, procedures, pregnancy/menstruation context
ORDER_LAB: FXIII activity (± antigen), inhibitor screen if indicated; CBC, fibrinogen, PT/INR, aPTT
GET_RESULTS: confirm low FXIII activity with otherwise normal routine coagulation tests
CHECK_ALLERGY: penicillin/latex and product-related allergies; plan latex-free supplies
CHECK_INTERACTION: review meds/OTC/herbals; counsel on avoiding aspirin/NSAIDs unless approved
PRESCRIBE: FXIII concentrate for prophylaxis (and plan for breakthrough bleeding), with monitoring

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 17-year-old male. You come because your parents say you’re “acting weird” and you can’t focus at school; you’ve been skipping classes and missing shifts sometimes. Speak tangentially, jump topics, and be evasive unless asked directly. Volunteer early: trouble focusing, people at school seem to talk/laugh about you, poor sleep because your mind won’t shut off, isolating from friends, irritability/snapping at family. If asked about mood/anxiety, mention feeling empty, not excited, anxious in crowds, grades dropping, can’t finish homework. If asked about psychotic symptoms, reluctantly admit: sometimes a voice comments when you’re alone; songs/ads feel like messages; thoughts get stuck/jump; mishearing noises as words; avoiding windows at night; spending hours online finding “patterns”; eating less because food doesn’t feel safe unless you see it made; less showering.

Withhold suicidal thoughts and the voice calling you worthless unless asked about self-harm/safety; then admit passive thoughts, no plan, and fear of being called “crazy.” Mention weed tried a few times, made paranoia worse. Push misconceptions: maybe autism, severe anxiety/panic, ADHD, seizures/brain tumor. Emphasize economic fears: “permanent record,” insurance/job risk, can’t afford treatment, missing work could get you fired. If the clinician shows empathy, soften slightly, but stay guarded and ask if this will be confidential.

comorbidities: Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome (CREBBP mutation) with mild-to-moderate intellectual disability
confounders: Cannabis use (THC positive) potentially contributing to psychosis and worsening paranoia
Intellectual disability and communication limitations from Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome complicating symptom characterization
Sleep deprivation/irregular sleep-wake cycle contributing to agitation and perceptual disturbances
Medication adverse effects (risperidone-associated hyperprolactinemia and metabolic changes) overlapping with other symptoms
Borderline prolonged QTc increasing risk with antipsychotic dose escalation or addition of QT-prolonging agents

correct_diagnosis.primary: Early-onset schizophrenia
required_evidence: Presence of at least 2 core psychotic symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, grossly disorganized/catatonic behavior, negative symptoms) with at least one being delusions, hallucinations, or disorganized speech
Functional decline (school, social, or self-care) compared with baseline
Total duration of illness at least 6 months for schizophrenia (or specify alternative diagnosis if shorter duration)
Symptoms not better explained by mood disorder with psychotic features (no sustained manic/depressive episodes accounting for the majority of the illness duration)
Symptoms not attributable to substance/medication or a medical/neurologic condition based on history/exam and available data
Early onset defined as onset before age 18

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically within reference range; no leukocytosis expected unless intercurrent infection/stress
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: Typically within reference range; consider mild hyponatremia only if psychogenic polydipsia suspected
expected_lab_results.CMP - BUN: Typically within reference range (assess hydration status)

medications: Risperidone 0.5 mg PO nightly; increase by 0.5 mg every 3-7 days as tolerated to 1-3 mg/day (target range for adolescents)
Olanzapine 2.5-5 mg PO nightly; titrate by 2.5-5 mg every 1 week to 10-20 mg/day (alternative if risperidone not tolerated/ineffective)
Aripiprazole 2 mg PO daily; increase to 5 mg after 2 days then 10 mg daily as tolerated (alternative with lower prolactin risk)
non_pharmacological: Assess and document suicide/self-harm risk, violence risk, and ability to care for self; arrange urgent psychiatric evaluation/inpatient admission if high risk or unable to ensure safety
Family psychoeducation and collaborative safety planning (warning signs, crisis contacts, means restriction)
Coordinated specialty care/early psychosis program referral (psychotherapy, family therapy, supported education/employment)
Supportive psychotherapy and CBT for psychosis when stable
Sleep hygiene and routine establishment; avoid cannabis/other substances
School accommodations and coordination with guardians and school counselors (as appropriate and consented)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 3-7 days after initiation or dose change; then every 1-2 weeks during titration, and at least monthly once stable (earlier if safety concerns)

must_include: Explain that schizophrenia is a brain-based condition causing psychotic symptoms (hearing/seeing things, unusual beliefs, disorganized thinking) and functional decline
Clarify diagnostic uncertainty early in illness and how duration and course affect the label (brief psychotic disorder vs schizophreniform vs schizophrenia)
Normalize and reduce stigma; emphasize that symptoms are treatable and early treatment improves outcomes
Rationale for antipsychotic use in first-episode psychosis and expected timeline (some improvement in days to weeks; fuller response over weeks)
Shared decision-making on medication choice; start low and go slow in adolescents
Key adverse effects to monitor: sedation, weight gain/metabolic effects (especially olanzapine), extrapyramidal symptoms/akathisia, prolactin-related effects (risperidone), orthostasis
Importance of adherence; do not stop abruptly without clinician guidance
When to seek urgent help: suicidal thoughts, severe agitation, rigidity/fever/confusion (possible NMS), severe restlessness, allergic reaction
Assess patient and family concerns about safety, stigma, school, and confidentiality; involve guardians appropriately given age and risk
Discuss risk assessment findings and safety plan; provide crisis resources and clear return precautions
Discuss substance use (especially cannabis) as a relapse/worsening factor and plan for reduction/cessation support
Sleep regularity, stress reduction, and structured daily routine to support recovery
Nutrition and activity counseling to mitigate antipsychotic-associated weight gain
Avoid alcohol/cannabis/illicit drugs; caution with driving/operating machinery if sedated
Encourage family support and engagement with early psychosis services

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting psychotic symptoms (hallucinations, delusions, disorganization), onset/duration/course, functional decline, mood symptoms, trauma, substance use (esp. cannabis), medical/neurologic symptoms, developmental history, meds, and collateral from family/guardian; include suicide/violence/self-care risk assessment.

Order baseline and secondary-cause/medication-safety evaluation appropriate for first-episode psychosis and adolescent antipsychotic start (e.g., CBC, CMP/LFTs, fasting glucose or HbA1c, fasting lipids, TSH, urine tox, pregnancy test if applicable; consider prolactin baseline if using risperidone; obtain vitals/weight/BMI/waist and BP; consider ECG if QT-risk or selecting QT-prolonging agent).
Review results to rule out medical/substance contributors, establish metabolic baseline, and identify contraindications/precautions before diagnosing/prescribing.

Provide a working diagnosis on the psychosis spectrum (brief psychotic disorder vs schizophreniform vs schizophrenia) based on duration, functional decline, and exclusion of mood/substance/medical causes; document diagnostic uncertainty early in illness.

Explain diagnosis in a stigma-reducing way; discuss prognosis and importance of early treatment; assess patient/family concerns, confidentiality/guardian involvement, school functioning, and substance use counseling.
Critical safety check before prescribing any antipsychotic or adjunct: confirm drug allergies and prior adverse drug reactions.
Critical safety check before prescribing: screen for drug–drug interactions (including OTC/supplements) and QT-prolonging combinations; consider interactions with substances.

Perform contraindication/precaution screening prior to prescribing: personal/family history of QT prolongation/syncope, seizure disorder, diabetes/obesity, hyperlipidemia, hepatic impairment, pregnancy, history of NMS, movement disorders, and current level of agitation/suicidality requiring urgent care.

Initiate evidence-based first-episode antipsychotic with shared decision-making; start low and go slow in adolescents; include dosing plan and monitoring plan (metabolic, EPS/akathisia, prolactin effects, orthostasis, sedation).

Explain treatment rationale and expected timeline; emphasize adherence and not stopping abruptly; review key adverse effects and urgent warning signs (suicidality, severe agitation, fever/rigidity/confusion for NMS, severe restlessness, allergic reaction); provide lifestyle counseling (sleep, routine, nutrition/activity, avoid alcohol/cannabis, driving caution if sedated), and encourage family support/early psychosis services.

Arrange close follow-up (within 1 week if starting medication or safety concerns; sooner if high risk), coordinate early psychosis program/therapy, and plan repeat vitals/weight and labs per metabolic monitoring schedule.
Close encounter with clear safety plan, crisis resources, and return precautions documented.

minimal_paths.description: Typical early-onset first-episode psychosis without immediate danger; outpatient initiation of antipsychotic plus monitoring and early psychosis referral.

steps: ASK: full psychosis history, duration/course, function, mood, substance use, medical causes, risk assessment, collateral

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 28-year-old man with sickle cell disease. You speak vaguely and a bit evasively, giving short answers unless pressed. Start by saying you have really bad pain all over—especially deep aching in lower back and legs since yesterday, coming in waves, now 9/10 and worse than prior crises. Also volunteer sharp chest pain with deep breaths, shortness of breath walking to the bathroom, low fever/chills, exhaustion/weakness.

If asked, admit mild cough with small yellow mucus, poor water intake, missed a couple hydroxyurea doses, dark urine, dizziness on standing, nausea/vomited once, intermittent headaches.

Do NOT volunteer: left upper belly fullness/soreness, fast breathing at rest, yellow eyes, puffy tender fingers, brain fog, decreased urination—only mention if specifically asked.

Resist disclosing cocaine use, running out of pain pills early, not using CPAP, and trouble affording refills/transport; deny or deflect unless the clinician is nonjudgmental and explains confidentiality and safety.

Express worries it could be a PE, heart attack, bad heartburn, or COVID/flu. If met with empathy, open up gradually and ask about cost and missing work.

Peripheral smear.value: Sickled cells and target cells present; polychromasia

medications: Ferrous sulfate anhydrous 325 mg PO daily
Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Hydroxyurea 1000 mg PO daily
Oxycodone 5 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for severe pain
Ibuprofen 600 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain (take with food)
Albuterol HFA 90 mcg/inhalation 2 puffs inhaled every 4-6 hours as needed for wheeze
confounders: Fever and leukocytosis may represent infection vs inflammatory response to vaso-occlusive crisis
Asthma can mimic or worsen dyspnea and complicate evaluation for acute chest syndrome
Current iron supplementation despite elevated ferritin may obscure anemia workup (risk of iron overload vs anemia of chronic inflammation)
Dehydration from poor oral intake can precipitate vaso-occlusive pain and elevate heart rate

correct_diagnosis.primary: Sickle cell anemia (HbSS) with chronic hemolytic anemia
acceptable_alternatives: Sickle cell anemia with baseline anemia (no acute crisis specified)
required_evidence: Known history of sickle cell anemia (HbSS) or prior confirmatory testing (hemoglobin electrophoresis/genotype)
Anemia on CBC (low hemoglobin/hematocrit and low RBC count)
Evidence of hemolysis: elevated total bilirubin with predominantly indirect (unconjugated) fraction
Appropriately elevated reticulocyte percentage consistent with hemolytic anemia (unless marrow suppression suspected)
Platelets often normal-to-high; WBC may be elevated at baseline in SCD

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Low (chronic anemia; commonly ~6–9 g/dL depending on baseline)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte percent: Elevated (reflecting compensatory erythropoiesis; if low/normal, consider aplastic crisis)
expected_lab_results.WBC count: Normal to elevated (baseline leukocytosis can occur; marked elevation suggests infection/inflammation)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to elevated (functional asplenia can be associated with thrombocytosis)
expected_lab_results.Direct bilirubin: Normal or mildly elevated with predominantly indirect hyperbilirubinemia

medications: Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Hydroxyurea 15 mg/kg PO daily (titrate to maximum tolerated dose with CBC monitoring) if recurrent vaso-occlusive crises, severe anemia, or other guideline-based indications
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO q6h PRN pain/fever (max 3 g/day; adjust for liver disease)
non_pharmacological: Avoid routine iron supplementation unless iron deficiency is confirmed (check ferritin/transferrin saturation when available); stop ferrous sulfate if no documented deficiency
Hydration and avoidance of triggers (dehydration, extreme temperatures, high altitude, smoking)
Vaccination optimization for functional asplenia (pneumococcal, meningococcal, Hib, annual influenza) and infection-prevention counseling

If symptomatic anemia or acute drop from baseline suspected: evaluate for hemolytic/aplastic crisis and consider transfusion per SCD protocols (not solely based on lab values without clinical context)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1–2 weeks for CBC/reticulocyte review and medication reconciliation; sooner (24–72 hours) if worsening anemia symptoms, fever, or pain escalation

must_include: Sickle cell anemia causes chronic anemia due to ongoing red blood cell breakdown (hemolysis)
Bilirubin can be elevated from hemolysis and may contribute to jaundice or gallstones
Reticulocytes rise as the body tries to replace destroyed red cells
Folic acid supports red blood cell production in chronic hemolysis
Iron is not routinely helpful in sickle cell anemia and can be harmful if taken without proven deficiency
Hydroxyurea benefits (fewer pain crises/acute chest episodes, improved anemia) and need for regular CBC monitoring; contraception/teratogenicity counseling if applicable
Review penicillin allergy history (rash/hives) and clarify severity; document and avoid penicillin-class drugs unless evaluated
Return precautions: fever ≥38.5°C (101.3°F), chest pain/shortness of breath, severe worsening pain, syncope, signs of severe anemia
Discuss transfusion risks/benefits if ever indicated (alloimmunization, iron overload) and importance of matched blood in SCD
Hydration, sleep, and trigger avoidance to reduce crises
Prompt evaluation for infections due to functional asplenia
Medication adherence and keeping follow-up labs/appointments

Review results to support diagnosis/physiology explanation (anemia, reticulocytosis, hyperbilirubinemia) and rule out alternative causes or urgent complications.
State assessment consistent with sickle cell anemia with chronic hemolysis (and whether stable vs acute complication based on symptoms/labs).

Prescribe appropriate therapy based on scenario: folic acid routinely; consider hydroxyurea if indicated and safe; avoid empiric iron unless deficiency confirmed; include monitoring plan.
Arrange follow-up for lab monitoring (CBC schedule if hydroxyurea), symptom reassessment, vaccination/infection prevention review, and hematology coordination.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 42-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start with: “My feet and hands have been getting weaker, and my voice has gotten really soft and breathy.” Early on, volunteer: foot slapping/tripping, weak airy voice you can’t project, and clumsy hands with buttons/jars.

If asked, share it’s slowly worsened over 2 years; grip gives out with grocery bags; voice fades with talking; occasional cough/choke on thin liquids; calves look smaller; stairs are harder but you can still walk.

Do not mention unless specifically asked about swallowing/chewing/breathing/sleep: smaller bites from chewing fatigue, dry mouth and shallow breathing at night, can’t whistle/blow balloons, food “going the wrong way,” neck fatigue holding head up.

Resist at first discussing avoidance of meals/social events and nighttime panic/shortness of breath; admit only if the clinician asks gently.

Express misconceptions: you thought reflux/allergies caused the voice, a pinched nerve caused foot drag, choking is eating too fast; you fear ALS. Bring up cost/job worries and downplay therapy as “optional.” If met with empathy, soften, admit fear, and answer more fully.

diagnosis.primary: distal myopathy with vocal cord weakness

correct_diagnosis.primary: Distal myopathy with vocal cord weakness (suspected genetic distal myopathy; consider MATR3-related distal myopathy/“vocal cord and pharyngeal weakness” phenotype)
acceptable_alternatives: MATR3-related distal myopathy with vocal cord and pharyngeal weakness (VCPDM)
Distal myopathy, adult-onset, genetic (pending neuromuscular workup)
Inclusion body myositis (IBM) with bulbar involvement (alternative if pattern fits)
Myotonic dystrophy type 1 (if myotonia/cataracts/cardiac features present)
Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) with distal weakness and vocal cord dysfunction (neurogenic alternative)
required_evidence: Progressive distal-predominant limb weakness (hands/feet) with relative proximal sparing early
Vocal cord weakness/hoarseness or dysphonia (± dysphagia/aspiration symptoms)
Myopathic pattern on exam/EMG (if available) rather than pure neuropathic pattern
Family history suggestive of inherited myopathy OR adult-onset slowly progressive course
Laboratory support: CK normal to mildly elevated (not markedly high as in necrotizing myopathy)
Exclusion of common mimics with available labs: normal TSH and vitamin B12 (or corrected deficiencies)

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal to mildly elevated (often < 1,000 IU/L); can be normal despite symptomatic weakness
expected_lab_results.AST: Normal or mildly elevated (may parallel muscle enzymes)
expected_lab_results.ALT: Normal or mildly elevated (may parallel muscle enzymes)
expected_lab_results.Vitamin B12: Typically normal (deficiency would suggest neuropathy/myelopathy contribution rather than primary myopathy)
expected_lab_results.25-OH Vitamin D: May be low/insufficient but would not fully explain distal weakness with vocal cord involvement

medications: Acetaminophen 650 mg PO q6h PRN pain (max 3,000 mg/day; consider lower max if chronic liver disease)
non_pharmacological: Immediate avoidance of succinylcholine and depolarizing neuromuscular blockers; add to allergy list and provide medical alert identification
Refer to neuromuscular specialist for diagnostic confirmation (genetic testing panel including MATR3; consider EMG and muscle MRI; muscle biopsy if needed)
ENT/laryngology evaluation with flexible laryngoscopy for vocal cord function; consider speech-language pathology for voice therapy and swallowing assessment
Aspiration risk mitigation: swallow evaluation if dysphagia/cough with meals; diet texture modification if indicated
Pulmonary evaluation if symptoms suggest respiratory involvement (spirometry including FVC; sleep assessment if nocturnal hypoventilation suspected)
Physical therapy focused on maintaining mobility, stretching, fall prevention, and energy conservation; occupational therapy for hand function and adaptive devices
If vitamin D deficient: supplement and recheck; optimize nutrition and safe exercise within tolerance
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neuromuscular and ENT follow-up within 2–4 weeks; sooner (within 24–72 hours) if aspiration symptoms, stridor, or rapidly progressive weakness

must_include: The condition is a muscle disease (myopathy) that preferentially affects distal muscles and can involve the vocal cords
Diagnosis is often confirmed with neuromuscular testing and genetic testing; labs may be only mildly abnormal
Discuss key alternative diagnoses and why further testing is needed
There is no single curative medication for most genetic distal myopathies; care focuses on symptom management, function, and safety
Plan includes neuromuscular referral, ENT evaluation, and therapy (PT/OT/SLP)
Warning signs requiring urgent care: choking/aspiration, stridor, breathing difficulty, rapidly worsening weakness
Explicitly state succinylcholine is contraindicated due to risk of dangerous hyperkalemia/rhabdomyolysis in underlying myopathy
Advise patient to inform all clinicians/anesthesia teams and to carry a medical alert
Clarify penicillin allergy history (childhood rash) and document reaction details
Fall prevention strategies and home safety modifications
Energy conservation and pacing; avoid overexertion that triggers prolonged weakness
Nutrition and vitamin D optimization; maintain activity within safe limits

Arrange timely follow-up to review testing, monitor progression/respiratory and swallowing status, and coordinate referrals; provide return precautions.
Close encounter after ensuring patient understanding, safety-netting, and plan documentation.

steps: ASK focused neuromuscular + bulbar/respiratory history and red flags
CHECK_ALLERGY and document penicillin reaction details
ORDER_LAB baseline myopathy/metabolic safety labs (CK, CMP incl K+, AST/ALT, TSH, CBC, HbA1c, vitamin D)
GET_RESULTS and interpret (acknowledge CK may be mild/normal)
ORDER_LAB/arrange EMG-NCS and neuromuscular genetic testing; referrals to neuromuscular and ENT; SLP/PT/OT plan
DIAGNOSE working diagnosis: suspected distal myopathy with vocal cord weakness; list differentials
EDUCATE diagnosis, non-curative focus, therapy/referral plan, urgent warning signs, succinylcholine contraindication + medical alert, lifestyle counseling
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP to review results and monitor swallowing/respiratory status

acceptable_variants.description: Variant that performs EMG/genetic testing planning before baseline labs, as long as labs are eventually ordered/reviewed and counseling is complete.
Variant that includes a drug_interaction_check only if prescribing symptomatic meds (e.g., vitamin D supplementation or reflux therapy) and completes contraindication_check before prescribing.
steps: PRESCRIBE (only after allergy_check and contraindication_check completed)

acceptable_variants.description: Variant that adds respiratory/sleep evaluation planning (spirometry, nocturnal hypoventilation screening) when symptoms suggest respiratory involvement; still meets core milestones.
steps: ASK (includes orthopnea, morning headaches, sleep quality)
ORDER_LAB (baseline labs + consider PFT order/referral if symptomatic)
